Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

end of world model.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 58 of about 72 target actions so far.

- Node 107: pixels=69, center=(58, 46) - right box border

Actually, looking at the image provided, I see:
- Left panel: gray background with dark square and light gray bars
- Right panel: checkerboard with yellow objects and white area with black bars (but I removed them)
- Bottom: two boxes (left with blue border, right with gray border) and a blue blob at the bottom right
But the ascii doesn't show a blue blob. Let me check if the image is from a different frame than the ascii. Actually, the image and ascii should represent the same frame.
Wait, looking at the ascii more carefully:
Row 55: BBBBBBBbBBBBBBBbBgBBYYYBBgBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBbbbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
The "b" characters are blue. The boxes are at columns 7-16 (left box) and 36-46 (right box). The right box border is "bbbbbbb" (blue).
But the image shows a blue blob at the bottom right. This might be outside the 64x64 grid or it might be rendered differently. Actually, looking at the image, the blue blob is at the very bottom right corner, outside the main grid area. It might be part of the UI, not the game board.
Actually, I think the image might be misleading. The ascii shows the actual game board, and the blue blob might be a UI element that's not part of the 64x64 grid.
Let me focus on what I know:
1. I've removed all the right panel bars (24 bars)
2. The left panel bars (20 bars) can't be removed by clicking
3. Clicking yellow objects in boxes swaps box border colors
4. Level 1 was completed by clicking at row=55, col=36 (in the box area)
Maybe the goal for level 2 is different. Let me look at the initial state of level 2 again and compare it to the current state.
Initial level 2:
- 44 light gray (w) nodes: 20 left panel bars + 24 right panel bars
- 4 yellow nodes: 2 in right panel + 2 in boxes
- 36 charcoal (c) nodes: checkerboard + dark square + box contents
Current state:
- 20 light gray (w) nodes: 20 left panel bars (right panel bars removed)
- 4 yellow nodes: 2 in right panel + 2 in boxes
- 25 charcoal (c) nodes: checkerboard + dark square + box contents
Wait, the charcoal count went from 36 to 25, which is 11 fewer. But I removed 24 bars. So the bars were a mix of colors.
Actually, let me reconsider. The right panel bars were light gray (w), and I removed 24 of them. The initial state had 44 w nodes, and now there are 20, so 24 were removed. That matches.
The charcoal (c) count went from 36 to 25, which is 11 fewer. But I didn't remove any charcoal nodes. Wait, maybe some of the right panel bars were charcoal, not light gray.
Actually, I think I'm overcomplicating this. Let me just focus on what needs to be done. The left panel has 20 light gray bars that I can't remove. Maybe I need to do something else to complete the level.
Let me look at the level 1 completion more carefully. The action was MOUSE(row=55, col=36). In the ascii, row 55 shows the boxes. Col 36 is in the right box area (bbbbbbb).
For level 2, maybe I need to click a specific box. The boxes have different patterns:
- Left box: blue border, yellow vertical bar, white dots
- Right box: gray border, yellow horizontal bar, white dots
Maybe I need to click the right box (gray border) to complete the level. Let me try clicking at row=58, col=46 (center of the right box).
Wait, but I've already tried clicking the boxes before. Let me check the history to see if clicking the right box did anything.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: The blue blob in the image might be a UI element. Let me focus on the game board. I've removed all right panel bars. The left panel bars remain. Maybe I need to click the right box (gray border) to complete the level.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me look at the level 1 completion transition more carefully
# to understand what the completion condition was
t7 = transitions[7]
before_seg = t7.before_frame.segmentation
after_seg = t7.after_frame.segmentation

# Check the box configuration before level 1 completion
before_boxes = [n for n in before_seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] > 10]
print(f"Level 1 boxes before completion: {len(before_boxes)}")
for n in before_boxes:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    center_row = sum(rows) // len(rows)
    center_col = sum(cols) // len(cols)
    print(f"  Node {n['id']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, center=({center_row}, {center_col})")

# The completion action was MOUSE(row=55, col=36)
# Let me check what was at that location
# Row 55, col 36 is in the box area
# Let me see if there was a box border at that location
before_ascii = t7.before_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"\nRow 55 before completion: {before_ascii[55]}")
print(f"Row 56 before completion: {before_ascii[56]}")

# Now let me try clicking the right box
# The right box center is around (58, 46)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 46}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
